SLX Contra Simple Regression¶

Upon investigating model performance with train/validate splits for the 2019 and 2025 snapshots, ridge and lasso regression models performed well compared to SLX in the poc notebook. Since SLX was only tried on pca with 20 components previously, this new insight warrants returning to SLX for comparison on respective reduction techniques.

Then, this notebook should be informative on the relevance of an SLX model in the face of high performance from simpler regression models.

In [150]:
import json
from itertools import product

import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns

from sklearn.metrics import pairwise_distances
from sklearn.decomposition import PCA
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler
from sklearn.model_selection import GroupKFold

from sklearn.cluster import KMeans
from sklearn.metrics import mean_squared_error, r2_score
import geopandas as gpd
from shapely.geometry import shape

from esda.moran import Moran
from esda.geary import Geary

from sklearn.decomposition import PCA, FactorAnalysis
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler
from sklearn.model_selection import cross_val_score, GridSearchCV
from sklearn.linear_model import Ridge, Lasso, ElasticNet

from scipy.stats import spearmanr, pearsonr

import warnings
warnings.filterwarnings("ignore")

RANDOM_STATE = 19

Setup Features and Target¶

In [151]:
# Load dataset and imd to create merged dataset with target variable

indicators_2019 = pd.read_parquet('combined_indicators_2019.parquet', engine='fastparquet')
indicators_2025 = pd.read_parquet('combined_indicators_2025.parquet', engine='fastparquet')

scores_2019 = pd.read_csv('imd_2019_scores.csv')
scores_2025 = pd.read_csv('imd_2025_scores.csv')

lsoa_lookup = pd.read_csv('lsoa_2011_2021_lookup.csv')

Perform Joins¶

In [152]:
scores_2019.rename(columns={'LSOA code (2011)': 'lsoa_code_11',
                           'Index of Multiple Deprivation (IMD) Score': 'imd_score'
                          }, inplace=True)

# lsoa codes were changed in 2021, so 2019 codes are converted to 2021 codes using the lookup table
scores_2019 = scores_2019.merge(lsoa_lookup[['lsoa_code_11', 'lsoa_code_21']], on='lsoa_code_11', how='right')
scores_2019.drop(columns=['lsoa_code_11'], inplace=True)
scores_2019.rename(columns={'lsoa_code_21': 'lsoa_code'}, inplace=True)


scores_2025.rename(columns={'LSOA code (2021)': 'lsoa_code',
                           'Index of Multiple Deprivation (IMD) Score': 'imd_score'
                          }, inplace=True)

scores_2019 = scores_2019[['lsoa_code', 'imd_score']]
scores_2025 = scores_2025[['lsoa_code', 'imd_score']]

# Merge features and target datasets on 'lsoa_code'
data_2019 = pd.merge(indicators_2019, scores_2019, on='lsoa_code')
data_2025 = pd.merge(indicators_2025, scores_2025, on='lsoa_code', how='left')
In [153]:
# Ensure features are consistent between datasets
common_features = list(set(data_2019.columns).intersection(set(data_2025.columns)))
data_2019 = data_2019[common_features]
data_2025 = data_2025[common_features]
In [154]:
# Check that now datasets have the same shape
data_2019.shape == data_2025.shape
Out[154]:
True

NaN, Null and Inf Values¶

In [155]:
if np.isinf(data_2019.select_dtypes(include=['number'])).sum().sum()>0:
    print("Warning: Infinite values found in 2019 dataset, replaced with NaN.")
    data_2019.replace([np.inf, -np.inf], np.nan, inplace=True) 

if np.isinf(data_2025.select_dtypes(include=['number'])).sum().sum()>0:
    print("Warning: Infinite values found in 2025 dataset.")
    data_2025.replace([np.inf, -np.inf], np.nan, inplace=True)

Warning: Infinite values found in 2019 dataset, replaced with NaN.
In [156]:
# 2019

missingness = data_2019.isna().sum().sort_values(ascending=False)
missingness_percent = (missingness / len(data_2019)) * 100
missingness_df_2019 = pd.DataFrame({
    'feature': missingness.index,
    'missing_count': missingness.values,
    'missing_percent': missingness_percent.values
})

data_2019 = data_2019.drop(columns=missingness_df_2019[missingness_df_2019['missing_percent'] > 20]['feature'])
data_2019 = data_2019.fillna(data_2019.median(numeric_only=True)) 

missingness_df_2019.sort_values('missing_percent', ascending=False).head(10)

In [157]:
# 2025

missingness = data_2025.isna().sum().sort_values(ascending=False)
missingness_percent = (missingness / len(data_2025)) * 100
missingness_2025_df = pd.DataFrame({
    'feature': missingness.index,
    'missing_count': missingness.values,
    'missing_percent': missingness_percent.values
})

data_2025 = data_2025.drop(columns=missingness_2025_df[missingness_2025_df['missing_percent'] > 20]['feature'])
data_2025 = data_2025.fillna(data_2025.median(numeric_only=True)) 

missingness_2025_df.sort_values('missing_percent', ascending=False).head(10)

In [158]:
# Ensure datasets still have the same shape after dropping features with high missingness
consistent_shape = data_2019.shape == data_2025.shape

print(f"Datasets have consistent shapes: {consistent_shape}")

if not consistent_shape:
    # redo the common features step to ensure both datasets have the same features after dropping those with high missingness
    common_features = list(set(data_2019.columns).intersection(set(data_2025.columns)))
    data_2019 = data_2019[common_features]
    data_2025 = data_2025[common_features]

Datasets have consistent shapes: False

Define Features and Targets¶

In [159]:
data_2019.set_index("lsoa_code", inplace=True)
data_2025.set_index("lsoa_code", inplace=True)

X_2019 = data_2019.drop(columns=['imd_score']).values
y_2019 = data_2019['imd_score'].values

X_2025 = data_2025.drop(columns=['imd_score']).values
y_2025 = data_2025['imd_score'].values

Setup GeoSpatial Aspects¶

Load Geo Shapes¶

In [160]:
lsoa_shapes = pd.read_csv("geography_lookup.csv")

# convert geojson string to geometry
lsoa_shapes["geometry"] = lsoa_shapes["geo_shape"].apply(lambda x: shape(json.loads(x)))
lsoa_shapes = lsoa_shapes[["lsoa_code", "geometry"]]

print(lsoa_shapes.head())

# merge modelling data to get full geo-spatial dataset
gdf = gpd.GeoDataFrame(lsoa_shapes, geometry="geometry", crs="EPSG:4326").merge(data_2025, on="lsoa_code", how="left")

   lsoa_code                                           geometry
0  E01014488  MULTIPOLYGON (((-2.581270401359658 51.47053350...
1  E01014693  MULTIPOLYGON (((-2.581328182358106 51.51617262...
2  E01033909  MULTIPOLYGON (((-2.598659490202402 51.45494441...
3  E01033903  MULTIPOLYGON (((-2.584357699648935 51.45285959...
4  E01032514  MULTIPOLYGON (((-2.59432877773493 51.409612444...

Get Distance Matrix¶

In [161]:
# Get centroid coordinates for distance matrix
coords = np.array(list(zip(gdf.geometry.centroid.x, gdf.geometry.centroid.y)))

# Compute distance matrix
distances = pairwise_distances(coords)

# Create adjacency: neighbors if within some distance threshold
threshold = 0.01  # roughly 700 meters with 1 ~ 111km
w = (distances < threshold).astype(float)

# row-standardize weights
w = w / w.sum(axis=1, keepdims=True)

Get Spatial CV Clusters¶

In [162]:
# Setup for spatial cross validation 

centroids = np.array(list(zip(gdf.geometry.centroid.x, gdf.geometry.centroid.y)))
n_clusters = 10
kmeans = KMeans(n_clusters=n_clusters, random_state=RANDOM_STATE).fit(centroids)
gdf["cluster_id"] = kmeans.labels_

groups = gdf["cluster_id"].values

Modelling¶

Define Spatial CV, Reductions and Eval Params¶

In [163]:
initial_feature_sets = {
    'pca_100': 100,
    'pca_75' : 75,
    'pca_50': 50,
    'pca_20': 20, 
    'pca_10': 10,
    'pca_5': 5,
    'fa_100': 100,
    'fa_75' : 75,
    'fa_50': 50,  
    'fa_20': 20, 
    'fa_10': 10,
    'fa_5': 5,
    'scaled': None,
    'none': None,
}

ridge_params = [0.1, 1.0, 10.0, 20.0, 50.0, 80.0, 100.0] # chosen using knowledge from 19/25 experiments  
lasso_params = [0.001, 0.01, 0.1, 1] # chosen as base


gkf = GroupKFold(n_splits=5)

Perform CV¶

In [164]:
def evaluate_models(
        feature_sets: dict = {'scaled': None},
        alphas: list = [0.01, 0.1, 1, 10, 100],
        elnet_ratio: list = [0.2, 0.5, 0.8],
        models: list = ['Ridge', 'SLX_Ridge'],
        feature_names: list | None = None
):

    results_row = []
    coefs_list = []

    for direction in ['2019 -> 2025', '2025 -> 2019']:

        if direction == '2019 -> 2025':
            X_cv = X_2019
            y_cv = y_2019
            X_val = X_2025
            y_val = y_2025
        else:
            X_cv = X_2025
            y_cv = y_2025
            X_val = X_2019
            y_val = y_2019       

        for reduction_config in feature_sets.keys():
            n = feature_sets[reduction_config]

            for model_type in models:
                if model_type not in ['Ridge', 'Lasso', 'Elastic_Net', 'SLX_Ridge', 'SLX_Lasso', 'SLX_Elastic_Net']:
                    continue

                if model_type not in ['Elastic_Net', 'SLX_Elastic_Net']:
                    elnet_ratio = [0]

                for a, lr_ratio  in product(alphas, elnet_ratio):

                    r2_scores = []
                    rmse_scores = []
                    spearmensr_scores = []
                    coeffs_cv = []

                    # CV loop
                    for train_idx, test_idx in gkf.split(X_cv, y_cv, groups=groups):

                        # Train/test split
                        X_train_raw, X_test_raw = X_cv[train_idx], X_cv[test_idx]
                        y_train, y_test = y_cv[train_idx], y_cv[test_idx]

                        scaler = StandardScaler()
                        X_train_scaled = scaler.fit_transform(X_train_raw)
                        X_test_scaled = scaler.transform(X_test_raw)

                        # Dimension reduction
                        if 'pca' in reduction_config:
                            pca = PCA(n_components=n)
                            X_train = pca.fit_transform(X_train_scaled)
                            X_test = pca.transform(X_test_scaled)
                            # Name features as PCA1, PCA2, ...
                            feature_names_curr = [f"PCA_{i+1}" for i in range(n)]
                        elif 'fa' in reduction_config:
                            fa = FactorAnalysis(n_components=n)
                            X_train = fa.fit_transform(X_train_scaled)
                            X_test = fa.transform(X_test_scaled)
                            feature_names_curr = [f"FA_{i+1}" for i in range(n)]
                        elif 'scaled' in reduction_config:
                            X_train = X_train_scaled
                            X_test = X_test_scaled
                            feature_names_curr = list(data_2019.drop(columns='imd_score').columns)
                        else:
                            X_train = X_train_raw
                            X_test = X_test_raw
                            feature_names_curr = feature_names if feature_names is not None else [f"X{i}" for i in range(X_train.shape[1])]

                        # Spatial features
                        WX_train = w[np.ix_(train_idx, train_idx)] @ X_train
                        WX_test  = w[np.ix_(test_idx, train_idx)]  @ X_train
                        X_train_slx = np.hstack([X_train, WX_train])
                        X_test_slx = np.hstack([X_test, WX_test])
                        if 'SLX' in model_type:
                            feature_names_curr = feature_names_curr + [f"W_{name}" for name in feature_names_curr]

                        # Model selection
                        if model_type == 'Ridge':
                            model = Ridge(alpha=a)
                            X_tr, X_te = X_train, X_test
                        elif model_type == 'Lasso':
                            model = Lasso(alpha=a, max_iter=10000, random_state=RANDOM_STATE)
                            X_tr, X_te = X_train, X_test
                        elif model_type == 'Elastic_Net':
                            model = ElasticNet(alpha=a, l1_ratio=lr_ratio, max_iter=10000, random_state=RANDOM_STATE)
                            X_tr, X_te = X_train, X_test
                        elif model_type == 'SLX_Ridge':
                            model = Ridge(alpha=a)
                            X_tr, X_te = X_train_slx, X_test_slx
                        elif model_type == 'SLX_Lasso':
                            model = Lasso(alpha=a, max_iter=10000, random_state=RANDOM_STATE)
                            X_tr, X_te = X_train_slx, X_test_slx
                        elif model_type == 'SLX_Elastic_Net':
                            model = ElasticNet(alpha=a, l1_ratio=lr_ratio, max_iter=10000, random_state=RANDOM_STATE)
                            X_tr, X_te = X_train_slx, X_test_slx

                        # Fit and predict
                        model.fit(X_tr, y_train)
                        y_pred = model.predict(X_te)

                        r2_scores.append(r2_score(y_test, y_pred))
                        rmse_scores.append(np.sqrt(mean_squared_error(y_test, y_pred)))
                        spearmens_score, _ = spearmanr(y_test, y_pred)
                        spearmensr_scores.append(spearmens_score)
                        coeffs_cv.append(model.coef_)

                    # Validation model
                    scaler = StandardScaler()
                    X_cv_scaled = scaler.fit_transform(X_cv)
                    X_val_scaled = scaler.transform(X_val)

                    if 'pca' in reduction_config:
                        pca = PCA(n_components=n)
                        X_cv_red = pca.fit_transform(X_cv_scaled)
                        X_val_red = pca.transform(X_val_scaled)
                    elif 'fa' in reduction_config:
                        fa = FactorAnalysis(n_components=n)
                        X_cv_red = fa.fit_transform(X_cv_scaled)
                        X_val_red = fa.transform(X_val_scaled)
                    else:
                        X_cv_red = X_cv_scaled
                        X_val_red = X_val_scaled

                    if model_type in ['SLX_Ridge', 'SLX_Lasso', 'SLX_Elastic_Net']:
                        WX_cv = w @ X_cv_red
                        X_cv_red = np.hstack([X_cv_red, WX_cv])
                        WX_val = w @ X_val_red
                        X_val_red = np.hstack([X_val_red, WX_val])

                    # Fit validation model
                    val_model = None
                    if model_type in ['Ridge', "SLX_Ridge"]:
                        val_model = Ridge(alpha=a)
                    elif model_type in ['Lasso', "SLX_Lasso"]:
                        val_model = Lasso(alpha=a, max_iter=10000, random_state=RANDOM_STATE)
                    elif model_type in ['Elastic_Net', 'SLX_Elastic_Net']:
                        val_model = ElasticNet(alpha=a, l1_ratio=lr_ratio, max_iter=10000, random_state=RANDOM_STATE)



                    val_model.fit(X_cv_red, y_cv)
                    val_coef = val_model.coef_

                    # Store results
                    results_row.append(
                        {
                            'feature_set': reduction_config,
                            'model': model_type,
                            'cv_rmse_std': np.std(rmse_scores),
                            'cv_rmse_mean': np.mean(rmse_scores),
                            'cv_r2_std': np.std(r2_scores),
                            'cv_r2_mean': np.mean(r2_scores),
                            'cv_spearmensr_std':np.std(spearmensr_scores),
                            'cv_spearmensr_mean': np.mean(spearmensr_scores),
                            'val_r2': r2_score(y_val, val_model.predict(X_val_red)), 
                            'spearmensr': spearmanr(y_val, val_model.predict(X_val_red))[0], 
                            'alpha': a,
                            'lr_ratio': lr_ratio,
                            'direction': direction,
                        }
                    )

                    # Build coefficient DataFrame rows with feature names
                    coeffs_cv_arr = np.array(coeffs_cv)
                    for idx, fname in enumerate(feature_names_curr):
                        coefs_list.append({
                            'feature_name': fname,
                            'validation_value': val_coef[idx],
                            'cv_mean': coeffs_cv_arr[:, idx].mean(),
                            'cv_spread': coeffs_cv_arr[:, idx].std(),
                            'model': model_type,
                            'alpha': a,
                            'feature_set': reduction_config,
                            'direction': direction
                        })

    results_df = pd.DataFrame(results_row)
    coefs_df = pd.DataFrame(coefs_list).set_index('feature_name')
    return results_df, coefs_df
In [ ]:
results_df, coefs_df = evaluate_models(
    feature_sets=initial_feature_sets,
    models=[
        'Ridge',
        'SLX_Ridge',
        'Lasso',
        'SLX_Lasso',
        'SLX_Elastic_Net',
        "Elastic_Net"
    ]
)

Evaluate¶

First the results dataframe is checked to see the models which scored highest on spearmensr and r squared on the validation set and during cv.

In [ ]:
results_df.sort_values(by='spearmensr', ascending=False).head(10)

796 scaled SLX_Lasso 0.465009 4.714234 0.020605 0.908261 0.012562 0.950801 -1.847608 0.479129 0.1 0 2025 -> 2019

792 scaled Lasso 0.278564 5.183360 0.033513 0.887466 0.015917 0.949202 0.768401 0.941226 1.0 0 2025 -> 2019
797 scaled SLX_Lasso 0.278563 5.183360 0.033513 0.887466 0.015917 0.949202 0.768401 0.941226 1.0 0 2025 -> 2019
828 none SLX_Lasso 1.002067 6.271439 0.095629 0.822681 0.020287 0.947676 0.392083 0.937703 10.0 0 2025 -> 2019
824 none Lasso 0.521041 5.320436 0.027981 0.883165 0.024022 0.946081 -0.056417 NaN 100.0 0 2025 -> 2019
372 scaled Lasso 1.553187 6.249293 0.065353 0.846951 0.014203 0.943354 0.702618 0.959504 1.0 0 2019 -> 2025
377 scaled SLX_Lasso 1.553187 6.249293 0.065353 0.846951 0.014203 0.943354 0.702618 0.959504 1.0 0 2019 -> 2025
376 scaled SLX_Lasso 1.906692 5.936801 0.096680 0.854443 0.009075 0.942621 0.605887 0.951765 0.1 0 2019 -> 2025

Here it seems Lasso regularisation dominates on metrics will almost all top scoring models being either a Lasso or SLX_Lasso model. Note also that these often happen with just a scaled dataset, likely because of Lassos ability to shrink coefficients to zero and so avoid dimensionality issues by performing feature selection. This is then probably possible because we know from previous experiments that some features correlate heavily with the target variable. Checking weather coefficients chosen is consistent throughout parameters.

However, this on its own is not to say Ridge or Elastic Net did badly, to observe these the result dataframe is queried with these model types specifically.

In [ ]:

    .sort_values(by="spearmensr", ascending=False) \
    .head(10)

Model Line Plots¶

Spearmens R on Validation Sets¶

Metrics obtained from the validation set are checked first.

In [ ]:
alpha_values = results_df['alpha'].unique()

for alpha_val in alpha_values:
    plt.figure(figsize=(10, 6))
    
    subset = results_df[results_df['alpha'] == alpha_val]

    sns.lineplot(
        data=subset,
        x='feature_set',
        y='spearmensr',
        hue='model',
        style='model',                
        markers=True,                 
        dashes=False,                 
        errorbar=None,
        palette="viridis"
    )
    
    plt.xticks(rotation=45)
    plt.title(f'Spearmens Rank Correlation on Validation Set Across Datasets (Alpha = {alpha_val})')
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.ylim(0, 1)
    
    plt.show()

As seen from the previous tables, Lasso regularisation outperforms other methods consistently apart from on high alpha values where they collapse. Also, SLX and standard Lasso seem to score the same, likely due to removing the same features. Perhaps spatial lags are completely shrunk, which should most certainly be checked.

Focusing on Ridge, the standard version here scores consistently higher than the SLX implementation.

CV R Squared Scores¶

CV R Squared scores are observed next. As noted earlier, R Squared on the validation set is misleading due to change in methodology between snapshots. Therefore, R Squared from CV is a better metric to consider.

Mean¶
In [ ]:
alpha_values = results_df['alpha'].unique()

for alpha_val in alpha_values:
    plt.figure(figsize=(10, 6))
    
    subset = results_df[results_df['alpha'] == alpha_val]

    sns.lineplot(
        data=subset,
        x='feature_set',
        y='cv_r2_mean',
        hue='model',
        style='model',                
        markers=True,                 
        dashes=False,                 
        errorbar=None,
        palette="viridis"
    )
    
    plt.xticks(rotation=45)
    plt.title(f'Mean R Squared Score From CV Across Datasets (Alpha = {alpha_val})')
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.ylim(0, 1)
    
    plt.show()

Here similar patterns are observed at first to spearmens rank, but as regularisation strength is increased, Lasso and Elastic Net fall apart. This could be from all coefficients being shrunk to zero. Otherwise, models seem to hover around 0.8 for feature sets with high component count which is a good score.

Spread¶

Spread was observed to be an issue in a previous notebook focusing on showcasing the viability of an SLX model, as such it is an important comparison point.

In [ ]:
alpha_values = results_df['alpha'].unique()

for alpha_val in alpha_values:
    plt.figure(figsize=(10, 6))
    
    subset = results_df[results_df['alpha'] == alpha_val]

    sns.lineplot(
        data=subset,
        x='feature_set',
        y='cv_r2_std',
        hue='model',
        style='model',                
        markers=True,                 
        dashes=False,                 
        errorbar=None,
        palette="viridis"
    )
    
    plt.xticks(rotation=45)
    plt.title(f'Standard Dev. R Squared Score From CV Across Datasets (Alpha = {alpha_val})')
    plt.tight_layout()
    
    plt.show()

Again, Lasso seems to consistently outperform Ridge - though scores on high alpha values come from all values being null so are misleading. On these high alpha values however, Ridge models do achieve less than 0.05 standard deviation which is only really approached by Elastic Net on low alpha values where it had been seen to do well in other metrics too. Otherwise, spread from Lasso on low alpha values is comparable.

Model Selection¶

Following initial exploration of model metrics, top performing models are selected next using multiple metrics.

In [ ]:
def pareto_front(df, cols):
    data = df[cols].values
    is_pareto = np.ones(data.shape[0], dtype=bool)

    for i, point in enumerate(data):
        if is_pareto[i]:
            is_pareto[is_pareto] = np.any(
                data[is_pareto] > point, axis=1
            ) | np.all(
                data[is_pareto] == point, axis=1
            )
            is_pareto[i] = True

    return df[is_pareto]

pareto_df = pareto_front(
    results_df,
    ['spearmensr', 'cv_r2_mean']
)
In [ ]:
pareto_df
Out[ ]:
feature_set model cv_rmse_std cv_rmse_mean cv_r2_std cv_r2_mean cv_spearmensr_std cv_spearmensr_mean val_r2 spearmensr alpha lr_ratio direction
372 scaled Lasso 1.553187 6.249293 0.065353 0.846951 0.014203 0.943354 0.702618 0.959504 1.0 0 2019 -> 2025
376 scaled SLX_Lasso 1.906692 5.936801 0.096680 0.854443 0.009075 0.942621 0.605887 0.951765 0.1 0 2019 -> 2025
377 scaled SLX_Lasso 1.553187 6.249293 0.065353 0.846951 0.014203 0.943354 0.702618 0.959504 1.0 0 2019 -> 2025
791 scaled Lasso 0.459823 4.733439 0.021605 0.907301 0.013528 0.950496 -2.147244 0.492141 0.1 0 2025 -> 2019
796 scaled SLX_Lasso 0.465009 4.714234 0.020605 0.908261 0.012562 0.950801 -1.847608 0.479129 0.1 0 2025 -> 2019
797 scaled SLX_Lasso 0.278563 5.183360 0.033513 0.887466 0.015917 0.949202 0.768401 0.941226 1.0 0 2025 -> 2019
823 none Lasso 0.694170 5.284380 0.038613 0.881867 0.017977 0.954215 0.392083 0.937703 10.0 0 2025 -> 2019

Seeing that Lasso models with an alpha of 0.1 and 1.0 dominate models in all metrics on a scaled dataset, it seems due to explore coefficients. Having said that though, Ridge should be returned to for further analysis.

Coeff Analysis¶

As mentioned before, the high and close performance of SLX Lasso and Standard Lasso regression models should bear investigating coefficient shrinkage.

In [ ]:
# Merge with coefs_df to get coefficients for these top 3
top_coefs = coefs_df.reset_index().merge(
    pareto_df[['model', 'alpha', 'feature_set', 'direction']],
    on=['model', 'alpha', 'feature_set', 'direction'],
    how='inner'
)

top_coefs['model_id'] = (
    top_coefs[['model','alpha','feature_set','direction']]
    .astype(str)
    .agg('_'.join, axis=1)
)

top_coefs.sort_values(by='validation_value')
Out[ ]:
feature_name validation_value cv_mean cv_spread model alpha feature_set direction model_id
2164 working_age_population -3.064449 -2.978045 0.264560 SLX_Lasso 0.1 scaled 2025 -> 2019 SLX_Lasso_0.1_scaled_2025 -> 2019
1825 working_age_population -3.031401 -3.004120 0.255376 Lasso 0.1 scaled 2025 -> 2019 Lasso_0.1_scaled_2025 -> 2019
469 working_age_population -3.029147 -3.147512 0.220520 SLX_Lasso 0.1 scaled 2019 -> 2025 SLX_Lasso_0.1_scaled_2019 -> 2025
461 landuse_greenfield_0 -2.731005 -2.366109 1.202224 SLX_Lasso 0.1 scaled 2019 -> 2025 SLX_Lasso_0.1_scaled_2019 -> 2025
522 pension_age_population -2.137486 -2.140452 0.156021 SLX_Lasso 0.1 scaled 2019 -> 2025 SLX_Lasso_0.1_scaled_2019 -> 2025
... ... ... ... ... ... ... ... ... ...
1857 mean_monthly_nwr_claims 11.534761 11.781883 1.927633 Lasso 0.1 scaled 2025 -> 2019 Lasso_0.1_scaled_2025 -> 2019

3729 rows × 9 columns

In [ ]:
# Loop through unique models
for model_id in top_coefs['model_id'].unique():
    subset = top_coefs[top_coefs['model_id'] == model_id].copy()
    
    # Sort features by validation coefficient magnitude
    subset['abs_val'] = subset['validation_value'].abs()
    subset = subset.sort_values('abs_val', ascending=True)

    subset = subset[subset['validation_value'].abs() >= 1]
    
    plt.figure(figsize=(8, 8))
    sns.barplot(
        x='feature_name', 
        y='validation_value', 
        data=subset, 
        palette='coolwarm'
    )
    
    plt.title(f'Coefficients For Model Config: {model_id}', fontsize=12)
    plt.xlabel('Coefficient value')
    plt.xticks(rotation=45)
    plt.ylabel('Feature')
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.show()

  sns.barplot(

Passing `palette` without assigning `hue` is deprecated and will be removed in v0.14.0. Assign the `x` variable to `hue` and set `legend=False` for the same effect.

  sns.barplot(

C:\Users\Python -test-\AppData\Local\Temp\ipykernel_26804\724702458.py:12: FutureWarning: 

Passing `palette` without assigning `hue` is deprecated and will be removed in v0.14.0. Assign the `x` variable to `hue` and set `legend=False` for the same effect.

  sns.barplot(

C:\Users\Python -test-\AppData\Local\Temp\ipykernel_26804\724702458.py:12: FutureWarning: 

Passing `palette` without assigning `hue` is deprecated and will be removed in v0.14.0. Assign the `x` variable to `hue` and set `legend=False` for the same effect.

  sns.barplot(

These coefficient plots are very revealing. Mean monthly 'no work requirements claims' for universal credit is consistently the most significant coefficient with a value usually above 10 whilst other coefficients absolute values don't even reach 5. Whatsmore, in all cases where the model is an SLX, spatial features are shrunk out apart from once on the number of community/social poi within 1 km which was with alpha at 0.1 (so on weaker regularisation).

Where does this leave us? Should prediction focus on utilising this dominant feature? How does this interact with previous fears of overfitting?

An assumption on how the model predicts from this could be that the dominant feature is enough to gauge the vague area where the deprivation score will sit and then the supporting features whose coefficients were relatively low offer correction and guidance to increase accuracy within the correct score area.

On the relevance of an SLX model, the lack of dominant "W" features is a bit dissapointing. An initial idea for why these are reduced out could be that the OSM features which aggregate within a boundary distance capture similar information. However, this is only the case for that dataset. The dominant UC feature does not have its spatial counterpart show up at all. Before concluding however, other modelling techniques coeffs should be explored too. Though Lasso models performed best, the highest scoring Ridge and Elastic Net were not too far behind.

(Note however that on these plots features whose absoloute coefficient value is less than 1 are hidden for interpretability)

Coeff Analysis Without Lasso¶

In [ ]:
pareto_df = pareto_front(
    results_df.query("model != 'Lasso' and model != 'SLX_Lasso'"),
    ['spearmensr', 'cv_r2_mean']
)
In [ ]:
pareto_df

    pareto_df[['model', 'alpha', 'feature_set', 'direction']],
    on=['model', 'alpha', 'feature_set', 'direction'],
    how='inner'
)

    top_coefs[['model','alpha','feature_set','direction']]
    .astype(str)
    .agg('_'.join, axis=1)
)

top_coefs.sort_values(by='validation_value')

1197 rows × 9 columns

In [ ]:
# Loop through unique models
for model_id in top_coefs['model_id'].unique():
    subset = top_coefs[top_coefs['model_id'] == model_id].copy()
    
    # Sort features by validation coefficient magnitude
    subset['abs_val'] = subset['validation_value'].abs()
    subset = subset.sort_values('abs_val', ascending=True)

    subset = subset[subset['validation_value'].abs() >= 1]
    
    plt.figure(figsize=(8, 8))
    sns.barplot(
        x='feature_name', 
        y='validation_value', 
        data=subset, 
        palette='coolwarm'
    )
    
    plt.title(f'Coefficients For Model Config: {model_id}', fontsize=12)
    plt.xlabel('Coefficient value')
    plt.xticks(rotation=45)
    plt.ylabel('Feature')
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.show()

Passing `palette` without assigning `hue` is deprecated and will be removed in v0.14.0. Assign the `x` variable to `hue` and set `legend=False` for the same effect.

  sns.barplot(

Here results are interesting again. Where FA was used, FA1 dominates in a fashion similar to coeff analysis on the Lasso models. It may be worth examining factor loadings to see if it holds the respective UC data.

On feature sets with just scaling, coefficient values are much more evenly spread than on Lasso. Though UC features are still the greatest in magnitude.

Only one SLX model was picked out here and it didn't select and spatial features.

Next this method is repeated once more except for this time focusing on SLX models specifically to see if spatial features are granted significant coefficients.

Coeff Analysis On SLX¶

In [ ]:
pareto_df = pareto_front(
    results_df.query("model != 'Lasso' and model != 'SLX_Lasso' and model != 'Ridge' and model != 'Elastic_Net'"),
    ['spearmensr', 'cv_r2_mean']
)
In [ ]:
pareto_df

In [ ]:
# Merge with coefs_df to get coefficients for these top 3
top_coefs = coefs_df.reset_index().merge(
    pareto_df[['model', 'alpha', 'feature_set', 'direction']],
    on=['model', 'alpha', 'feature_set', 'direction'],
    how='inner'
)

top_coefs['model_id'] = (
    top_coefs[['model','alpha','feature_set','direction']]
    .astype(str)
    .agg('_'.join, axis=1)
)

top_coefs.sort_values(by='validation_value')
Out[ ]:

In [ ]:
# Loop through unique models
for model_id in top_coefs['model_id'].unique():
    subset = top_coefs[top_coefs['model_id'] == model_id].copy()
    
    # Sort features by validation coefficient magnitude
    subset['abs_val'] = subset['validation_value'].abs()
    subset = subset.sort_values('abs_val', ascending=True)

    subset = subset[subset['validation_value'].abs() >= 1]
    
    plt.figure(figsize=(8, 8))
    sns.barplot(
        x='feature_name', 
        y='validation_value', 
        data=subset, 
        palette='coolwarm'
    )
    
    plt.title(f'Coefficients For Model Config: {model_id}', fontsize=12)
    plt.xlabel('Coefficient value')
    plt.xticks(rotation=45)
    plt.ylabel('Feature')
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.show()

  sns.barplot(

Patterns repeated here seem identical to the previous sections except for here some PCA appears which tend to follow the scaled datasets feature coeff distributions as seen before. Also, the firs Ridge and Elastic Net SLX models do make use of some spatially weighted features which is good to see.

With this in mind it seems even more pressing to investigate factor loadings on FA.

Factor Loadings¶

In [ ]:
fa_10 = FactorAnalysis(n_components=10, random_state=RANDOM_STATE) 
fa_75 = FactorAnalysis(n_components=75, random_state=RANDOM_STATE)

# 2019
X_scaled_2019 = StandardScaler().fit_transform(X_2019)
X_fa_10_2019 = fa_10.fit_transform(X_scaled_2019)
X_fa_75_2019 = fa_75.fit_transform(X_scaled_2019)

loadings_10 = pd.DataFrame(fa_10.components_.T, index=list(data_2019.columns), columns=[f'Factor {i+1}' for i in range(10)])
loadings_10.sort_values(by='Factor 1', ascending=False).head(5)

mean_monthly_nwr_claims 0.954090 -0.182514 -0.191281 -0.140756 2.894797e-08 -2.480003e-09 -1.849837e-11 -6.170919e-12 5.121644e-10 -2.283311e-10 ... -6.348751e-13 2.674287e-13 -6.343191e-13 3.649566e-13 1.849253e-13 -1.050656e-12 8.236015e-14 -4.132459e-13 8.987284e-13 9.105738e-14

5 rows × 75 columns

As expected, the dominant Factor 1 feature is , made up mostly of UC related features - which alligns with findings from Lasso coefficients.

A heatmap is produced below to give an idea of how the dataset is squeezed to the different factors.

In [ ]:
# Factor loadings
for loadings in [loadings_10, loadings_75]:
    threshold = 0.2
    loadings_masked = loadings.where(loadings.abs() >= threshold)
    
    # Heatmap
    plt.figure(figsize=(12, 8))
    sns.heatmap(loadings_masked, cmap='coolwarm', center=0)
    plt.title("Factor Loadings Heatmap")
    plt.show()

Immediately, it can be seen that Factor 2 holds the majority of the datasets features which if the first Ridge and Elastic Net SLX models mentioned above are recalled, is a dropped feature. Meaning, the majority of the datasets features were not used by those models. Subsequent factors have constantly reduced number of feature loadings.

Factor 3 is checked in more detail since those models made use of it the most beyond Factor 1 as well as including its spatially weighted version.

In [ ]:
loadings_10.sort_values(by='Factor 3', ascending=False).head(30)
Out[ ]:

count_transport_car_1250 -0.057944 0.859286 0.423309 0.095053 -0.136277 -0.104036 -0.080858 -0.066931 -0.019416 -0.094678
count_public_amenities_environment_250 -0.114124 0.727184 0.419703 0.327506 0.023633 -0.000251 0.045522 0.214280 -0.035999 0.133484

Bar but one, these are all OSM features but what is perhaps more interesting, they have higher loadings on Factor than 3. This is somewhat perplexing and could bear looking into further, but being mindfull of the this notebook's purpose, maybe thats best left as further work.

Summary¶

In summary this notebook shows a few things:

  1. Simple regression models outperform SLX
  2. Lasso outperfrorms Ridge
  3. UC features are highly predictive and are often dominant in regression models.

On 1:

In most cases simple regression models either outperformed their spatial counterparts and when SLX did do well, spatially weighted features were often regularised out. Though there were two models seen at the end: Ridge SLX with an alpha of 10 and Elastic Net SLX with an alpha of 0.1, both being on teh FA 10 dataset. Apart from doubts on overfitting, model stability or any other case beyond the metrics of this notebook, the case can be made that non-spatial regression models work better. This is likely due to many features already implicitly holding spatial relationships, reducing the marginal benefit of adding spatial lags.

On 2:

Lasso regularised models perform best on almost all metrics. After some coefficient analysis it is seen that Lasso does best when shrinking the scaled dataset of roughly 400 features down to about 10 which suggests that the underlying relationship is sparse, with predictive power being concentrated in a small subset of features. This is in part likely due to high multicolinierity. In this sense, many of the original variables likely act as proxies for a smaller number of latent factors. Lasso effectively reduces down to just these latent factors whereas Ridge attempts to do the same whilst retaining some noise and redundancy.

On 3:

The bets performing models and even some less so select a UC feature or a reduced feature which is highly loaded with a UC feature with a coefficient which is an order of magnitude above all other coefficients. In thi sense, UC features are disproportionately predictive compared to other features. This kind of reliance may warrant further investigation and could raise issues of robustness if that one feature becomes unavailable.

Next Steps:

  1. Repeat experiments on tree based models with the extended dataset.
  2. Perform modelling experiments with a dataset omitting UC features. Observe how this impacts performance, coeffs and weather it gives some models more relevance.
